abstract:
Following a decade of continuous crisis management, the democratic legitimacy of emergency governance within the European Union has come under intense scrutiny. Current scholarship is largely critical, highlighting a distinct trend of "transnational crisis exploitation." In this view, elite appeals to exceptional circumstances serve to consolidate asymmetric power, bypass democratic norms, and inadvertently fuel Eurosceptic backlash. Despite these critiques, existing literature rarely examines whether citizens actually perceive themselves as disempowered by the EU’s emphasis on urgency and technocratic intervention. 

The Covid-19 pandemic, characterized by its relative symmetry and simultaneity across Member States, provides a unique empirical window into public attitudes toward emergency politics. By synthesizing the implications of emergency rule with the literature on transnational cleavages, we utilize survey data from 15 Member States regarding pandemic responses at both the national and EU levels to test competing hypotheses. Our findings reveal that perceptions of crisis management are primarily shaped by pre-existing views on European integration and democratic health. Furthermore, the results suggest that the transnational cleavage remains the fundamental driver—and delimiter—of Euroscepticism during periods of instability. While perceptions vary across different dimensions of governance, we find no evidence of widespread public disillusionment specifically triggered by the EU's emergency measures.
